title: Dog Soft Spot on Top of Head: Understanding the Fontanel
meta_desc: Discover what a fontanel (soft spot) on a dog's head means, its urgency level, and when to consult a veterinarian for your pet's safety.
content_html:
What is a Fontanel?

A fontanel, often referred to as a "molera," is a soft spot on the top of a dog's skull where the bony plates have not fully fused together. This condition is most common in toy and small breeds, such as Chihuahuas. In many puppies, these spots are normal and will gradually close as they mature, though in some dogs, they may remain open for life.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for an open fontanel is generally Low. In most cases, it is a known anatomical feature and not an immediate medical crisis. However, it is important to be cautious; the brain is more vulnerable to injury in this area. You should seek veterinary attention if the spot appears to be bulging, if your dog sustains a head injury, or if they exhibit neurological symptoms like seizures, circling, or extreme lethargy.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-quality photo of the top of your dog's head can be incredibly helpful for a triage professional. A clear image allows us to see the size of the area and the condition of the skin over the fontanel. This helps in determining whether the area looks typical for the breed or if there are signs of swelling or fluid buildup that might require a more urgent diagnostic workup.

faqs.question: Will my puppy's soft spot eventually close?
faqs.answer: In many puppies, the fontanel will close by the time they are three to six months old. However, in some toy breeds, a small opening may persist throughout their adult life.

faqs.question: Does a soft spot mean my dog has hydrocephalus?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. While an open fontanel can be associated with hydrocephalus (water on the brain), many dogs live healthy lives with a persistent soft spot and no underlying health issues.

faqs.question: What precautions should I take for a dog with a fontanel?
faqs.answer: You should protect your dog from head trauma. Avoid rough play with larger animals, be careful not to drop them, and use a harness instead of a collar to prevent unnecessary pressure near the head and neck.
